content.claim: In a Hungarian micro-region, settlements with disproportionately high Roma population shares exhibited corresponding school-level Roma enrollment disparities; neighboring settlements perceived successful schools' pupil 'pull' as a threat to their own enrollment, creating informal inter-municipal competition that distorts simple geographic-efficiency models.
content.context: Fresh live research through the School network planning lens
limitations: Limited to a single micro-region; generalization to national school-planning context is speculative
Underlying causal mechanisms (segregation preference, funding incentives, informal stigmatization) not disaggregated
Does not quantify the magnitude of competitive enrollment effects relative to distance-based factors
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
